# 1\. Identifying and Defining {#1.-identifying-and-defining}

## 1.1 Problem Statement {#1.1-problem-statement}

 My project addresses a clear lack of sports arcade ski games. Skiing is an immensely popular sport which struggles for a representation in the sports arcade gaming market. Sport’s arcade enthusiasts have been lacking a proper slope arcade game since Microsoft’s 1991 ski free, which has significant control flaws that makes the game feel shallow and unplayable emphasising the opportunity that that project captivates upon.  
   
The opportunity is significant as there is a clear lack of competition, and there is a significant audience to provide a playerbase for a sports arcade ski game. A google search of “2D pixel ski Game” showcases the clear lack of competition as the second result is Microsoft's game from 1991, indicating that there is minimal competition in the snowy sport arcade market, and that there is a clear opportunity to develop a product to fulfill the market gap.

It’s evident that there is a clear gap in the sports arcade market for a game that can properly address the shortcomings of existing projects. The lack of smooth and satisfying controls significantly highlights the need for an enjoyable sports arcade experience. An example of the importance of smooth and satisfying controls can be seen in the success of the Marvel Spiderman game where the high quality control set directly led to the game’s success.

## 1.2 Project Purpose and Boundaries {#1.2-project-purpose-and-boundaries}

The game is going to fulfill the gap in the sports arcade market by delivering a fun, interactive and replayable game experience to snow sports enthusiasts. The system will be an online experience that will be accessible to the largest proportion of devices through using any modern internet browser.  The purpose of my project is to beat existing examples on their movement and limited gameplay and deliver an effective roguelike sports arcade hybrid that captivates the target market.

## 1.3 Stakeholder Requirements {#1.3-stakeholder-requirements}

The stakeholders within this project include gamers, skiers and people interested in a simple but fun arcade winter sports game. The target market is broad and includes a wide variety of backgrounds, ages and experiences (particularly varying experience with gaming controls). An example of a target gamer could be someone who is interested in skiing, searching for a game to fill some time. 

The expectations, needs and requirements of the end user significantly influenced the direction of my project by highlighting certain functional and non functional requirements.  An example of this is the expectations of a simple control set that is easy to pick up suiting the broad target market with varying gaming skills. It’s important that their expectations surrounding the game's gameplay, visual and auditory quality is met to effectively appeal to the target audience. The stakeholder expectations for this project require effective implementation of game logic and output to tick functional and non functional requirements which directly influenced this project’s direction. 

## 1.4 Functional Requirements {#1.4-functional-requirements}

The system must achieve

* A login and saving system to safely store user data about highscores, characters and items.  
* Users must be able to play a fun arcade sports game  
* The system must have random item logic that provides a sense of rarity and satisfaction to the player.  
* The system must be able to track player’s position and direction  
* The game must be able to respond to user inputs and control the player accordingly  
* The game must have an obstacle collision system that properly detects collisions of the player’s hitbox.  
* The system must clearly show the score and highscore of the user.  
* The game must have 4 clear levels of difficulty that scale overtime.  
* The system must clearly show what each item does, in a concise and easy to understand manner.

## 1.5 Non-Functional Requirements {#1.5-non-functional-requirements}

The system will ideally

* The game should load within 3 seconds.  
* The system should respond quickly to the players actions  
* The game should maintain smooth and responsive controls that are easy to pick up  
* The system must be reliable and not crash  
* The game should handle errors gracefully and provide clear error messages.  
* The game must be clear and understandable.  
* Pretty and clean visuals with handmade graphics  
* Have appealing and appropriate audio

## 1.6 Constraints {#1.6-constraints}

The limitations that will directly affect my project include the time constraints, 2D graphics limitations and the technical limitations of my skills. 2D graphics and my skill limitation mean that in the time provided my project must have a limited scope and focus on certain goals to ensure a completed project.  Time and technical skills directly limit the scope of my project, as I have to prioritise certain functions of my game that best appeal to the end user. 

## 1.7 Requirements Analysis and Prioritisation {#1.7-requirements-analysis-and-prioritisation}

	  
Requirements have been prioritized to best encapsulate the target audience and meet timeframe restrictions. I made trade offs surrounding my game due to constraints including my technical skills and the timeframe for completion to better execute other requirements creating a more addicting and fun game in comparison to completing everything to a low quality. For instance, I prioritised core gameplay rather than story to best appeal to the target audience who are not interested in a story. The prioritisation of requirements also aligns with the identified problem of existing alternatives which have minimal control sets which directly impacts the replayability and overall quality of the game reinforcing the importance of properly executing important requirements.

# 2\. Research and Planning {#2.-research-and-planning}

## 2.1 Development Methodology {#2.1-development-methodology}

I will apply the WAgile approach in my software project due to its ability to best adapt to a mandatory deadline but adapting requirements based on testing and further research. The clear flexibility will support my project by allowing the iterative design in certain phases but also the clear completion of alternative steps in other phases. The development approach of Wagile allows me to best incorporate the functional and non functional requirements within the time constraints present.

## 2.2 Tools and Technologies {#2.2-tools-and-technologies}

| Tool / Technology | Purpose  | Justification |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Javascript | The front end language for the project that drives the game. | Works on a large proportion of devices |
| Canvas (Javascript inbuilt game design module) | To provide the canvas for the game. | I have previous skills and knowledge of using Canvas and is the most suitable way to employ games on the web |
| HTML | To provide the core structure of the website. | Works on a large proportion of devices |
| CSS | To provide any styling that may be required for the website . | Works on a large proportion of devices |
| Vercel | To host the website and database system. | The simplest way to host a website that isn’t static. |
| Supabase |  Database system for login, signup and saving progress for the user. | Has integration with Vercel and is a simple database system that is suitable for the demands and scale of this project. |

The selection of JS canvas as my core game development tool came to previous skills and the accessibility of my game.  The selection of JS canvas allows the largest proportion of devices to access my game and as a result significantly impacted the other tools that I will employ to bring this project to life. Further requirements narrowed down the web hosting and database system for this project where I chose Vercel for its simple database integration and deployment for my dynamic web game . An example of a popular sports arcade game executed with the Javascript Canva can be seen in Microsoft Edge Surf, showcasing how a game of a similar scale is executable with the game development tools. It’s clear that the selection of Javascript Canvas as my core game development tool significantly influenced the other tools that I plan to employ to complete the project.

My choice of development tools for this project prioritise efficient and effective development as I selected well known tools that I have had an experience with to allow the immediate implementation of game logic and development. By using these tools I do not need to learn the game development or structure of the code from scratch which allows me to best execute my game’s requirements without the tools being a constraint. 

## 2.3 Gantt Chart / Timeline {#2.3-gantt-chart-/-timeline}

![][image1]

### 

Time was allocated across the planning, development, testing and evaluation sections to ensure the efficient completion of the project within the given time constraints. I dedicated almost half of my time for the planning portion to clearly outline the skeleton of the game to minimise wastage in the Development phase which lasts around 30%\~ of the remaining time. Major changes at this stage would significantly damage the quality of the final game which is why it’s important to clearly lay out how my game will work in the planning stage. The last 20%\~ of my time is dedicated to seeking final testing, feedback and evaluation to tie up this project. The structured time allocation deliberately dedicates a larger proportion of my time to the more time consuming and challenging parts of the game development process.

## 2.4 Communication Plan {#2.4-communication-plan}

Peer and teacher feedback will be obtained in class and immensely shift the design of features and development throughout the process of the project. I will incorporate changes and follow suggestions to best achieve my goals for this game project which ensures the quality of the game for the target audience.  An example of the importance of feedback can be seen within Valve’s playtesting where feedback from game testers led to the creation of Glados as the villain which is now one of the most recognisable antagonists in the gaming world showcasing the importance of feedback to drive successful game development. 

## 2.5 Resource Allocation Justification {#2.5-resource-allocation-justification}

 The effective resource allocation of time, software tools and human input was essential to drive the success of my project. The usage of human input has been timetabled to properly support the development of my project to drive success. I will utilise the available software tools to efficiently complete my goals within the timeframe. For example, I will use Vercel to effectively and efficiently deploy my project with its inbuilt integrations to a database to match my requirements within the provided time constraints. It's clear that my effective utilisation of software tools, human feedback and time will lead to a successful outcome for this project.

## 3.2 Structure Chart![][image5] {#3.2-structure-chart}

Updated Structure Chart

## 3.3 IPO Chart {#3.3-ipo-chart}

| Input  | Process | Output |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Enter the URL and launch game window | Run menu initialisation logic | Game window displays the title screen |
| Click the title menu buttons | Run the relevant logic for the next page.  | Display the relevant screen. Account Page, or the difficulty selector page or the Credits page  |
| Click the signin or signup option | Prepare to take user Input | Display the sign in/signup page |
| Signin or signup page fillout | Check if the input passes the relevant checks and display the password visibility as referenced by the checkbox. | Output red error text if it does not pass the input checks or take the user to the next page in the process. |
| Select difficulty, starting skis and starting item through click | Change the difficulty of the game. As well as set starting skis and the starting item. | Change highlighting inside the selection box to identify to the user that the difficulty has changed. And change the dropdown thumbnail in the case of starting item |
| Key presses, in the core gameloop | Calculate momentum, position and direction of character. | Display character movement affected by input, and the trail showing previous directions to provide a sense of progressional change in movement. Apply character animations to blend the process and demonstrate the direction of the skier more clearly to the user. |
| Click Main Menu | Clear Game Process, Update Highscore and add any unlocked items to the starting item pool for the logged in user. | Returns the screen to the title screen, (logged in flag does skip the login process again however) |

## 3.4 Data Dictionary\*  {#3.4-data-dictionary*}

| Variable | Data Type | Format for Display | Size in Bytes | Size for Display | Description | Example | Validation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| speed | Float | xxxxx.xx m/s | 4 bytes | 4 | The speed of the character | 5000 | Must be a valid float gained incrementally. |
| direction | String | XX  | 2 bytes | 2  | The direction of the character | NW | Must be a valid direction to indicate the direction of the character. |
| turning | Bolean | xxxx | 1 byte | 4  | A boolean to check if the character is turning | True | Must be a valid boolean. |
| items | Dict | {xxxx:xxx, xxxx:xxx} | 240 bytes+^ | 240+^ | A dictionary storing all of the user’s items that they have gathered during their run. | {Medkit: Medkit\_Class\_reference ,  Red\_skis:Class\_object\_reference} | Must be valid items. |
| turn\_count | Int | xxxx | 2 bytes | 3+ | The amount of turns by the player in a run | 345 | Must be a valid int, gained incrementally. |
| trees\_missed | Int | xxxxx | 2 bytes | 3 \+ | The amount of trees missed by the player in a run | 45 | Must be a valid float, gained incrementally. |
| Score | Int | xxxxxx… | 2 bytes | 3 \+ | The score of the game with items applied gained by speed and time alive | 681 | Must be a valid int, gained incrementally. |
| High Score | Int | xxxxxx… | 2 bytes | 3+ | The highest score of the player with items applied gained by speed and time alive | 1800 | Must be fetched from the saved database. |
| Time Alive | Time (Str) | xx:xx | 5 bytes | 5 | The time that the run has been going on for. | 2:05 | Must be a valid time, gained incrementally. |
| top\_speed | Float | xxxxx.xx m/s | 4 bytes | 8+ | The highest speed of the run | 500.62m/s | Must be a valid float, gained incrementally. |
| rocks\_dodged | Int | xxxxxx | 2 bytes | 3+ | The amount of rocks dodged by the player in a run | 41 | Must be a valid int, gained incrementally. |
| Total Items | Int | xxx | 2 bytes | 3+ | The total number of items collected in a run | 12 | Must be a valid int, gained incrementally. |
| Logged\_in | Bolean | xxxx | 1 byte | 4+ | The logged in flag to check if there is an account. | True | Must be a valid boolean set by the system  |
| Difficulty | String | xxxxxxxx | 8 bytes | 8 | The difficulty that affects how hard or easy the game is | Blizzard | Must be one of the valid difficulties.  |
| starting\_item | string | xxxxxxxxxxxx | 12 bytes | 12 | The item that you start the run with. | Medkit | Must be a valid unlocked item |
| username | string | xxxxxxxxxxxx | 12 bytes | 12 | The username to login to gather the progress and highscores of a user. | Skiman | Must follow the valid format. It must not include spaces and be greater than 2 characters. |
| password | string | xxxxxxxxxxxx | 12 bytes | 12 | The password to login to gather the progress and highscores of a user. | password1@ski | Must follow the valid format. It must include a number and symbol and be greater than 6 characters. |
| show\_password | Bolean | xxxx | 1 byte | 4 | To display the password in a readable format for the user. | False | Must be done by the system. |
| unlocked\_items | Dict | {xxxx:xxx, xxxx:xxx} | 240 bytes+^ | 240+^ | A dictionary storing all of the new items that they have unlocked during their run. | {Medkit: Medkit\_Class\_reference} | Must be valid items. |

\*Subject to change of course   
^ No clear measurement but generally can exceed high byte counts  
% UPDATED IS IN APPENDICIES

## 3.5 UML Class Diagram {#3.5-uml-class-diagram}

## 

### ![][image6]

The character will be a class because I can effectively employ OOP principles to best check all of the values involved in the movement of the character and output appropriate animations and positions back to the user. The item dictionary (inside the character’s saved data) holds items inside a dict which have 3 subclass types that are inherited from the previous “Item” class that guide their abilities and information to change the game for the user.  An example of this is the Mobility Subclass of Item where it checks for the specific mobility changes to affect the gameplay by slowing down or speeding up the character.

## 

## Updated UML Class Diagram {#updated-uml-class-diagram}

![][image7]

.

# 4\. Producing and Implementing {#4.-producing-and-implementing}

## 4.1 Development Process {#4.1-development-process}

The software solution was built on an Wagile development system.   
I specifically chose the agile development methodology for its adaptability and ability which allowed me to best dedicate time to where was needed, however time constraints at the latter end of my project meant that I was forced to dedicate most of my time fixing integration bugs. Potentially, using another software development methodology would have allowed me to deliver a more polished final product.

 The employment of object oriented principles enabled the smooth linkage of variables and functions to fulfill all of my requirements. OOP is an invaluable tool within javascript that allows you to effectively store numerous variables in one object and pass that around keeping your codebase much tidier and neater. OOP principles ended up being crucial within my project, as the storing of values in the character class drove my movement and allowed me to store items. OOP design was central to the development process because of the numerous benefits that it brings including readability and ease of use.

The reuse of code chunks allowed me to save considerable time during the development phase of my project. Modular development through functions, and the separation of my code files into their respective purpose simplified the core gameloop and allowed me to bugfix much easier throughout the development process of my project. An example of the reuse of code can be seen in the drawing of my character where I reused the same chunk of code copy and pasted but with different character sizing to draw both characters. The use of functions, code reuse and modular development were crucial to the development process as they allowed me to efficiently develop all of the required functionality and features for my project.

## 4.2 Key Features Developed {#4.2-key-features-developed}

| Core Feature  | Explanation | Justification |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Inbetween Menu | A premenu before the gameplay loop after logging in that allows you to choose your starting item out of your unlocked items and the difficulty of the game.  | It allows the user to select a difficulty and have greater control of their gameplay through item choice. This allows for powerful synergies and can adapt the game to suit your personal style. |
| Movement | The movement system for the character | Movement is crucial for my game, as it differentiates myself from existing ski arcade games present on the market |
| Items | Items that modify or change the game to give it variety. Starting items allow the player to make strategic choices | The items increase replayability and allow the user to change the game closer to their preferences. Starting items allow players to work towards completion of something which boosts the addictiveness and fun factor of my game.  |
| Difficulty | The difficulty system has 3 levels that grow in difficulty, although all 3 are not easy | Difficulty allows replayability and customisation as they make it easier or harder based on their preferences and experience with similar games. |
| Score/ Highscore | The scoring system for the game, which is a numerical digit that can be affected by items and speed of the user. | It provides a clear indication of your progress within the game and creates the incentive to go faster and survive for longer periods of time. The highscore allows you to compare each and every run to your best. |
| Unlock system | Items on logged in accounts are all locked besides the starter item which is a medkit.  | Builds a sense of progression and progress to work towards within my game. |
| End Screen | The end system provides the user with clear information about their run and clearly communicates that they died.  | Allows the user to understand how good or bad and provides clear clarification that they did  die and didn’t reload the page. |
| Obstacle System | An obstacle system ensures that a random array of obstacles challenge the player | The game would have no variety if a tree was always in the same place, it adds to replayability to have completely new scenarios for the player to dodge and evade from every time. |

## 4.2.1 Back-End Engineering Contribution {#4.2.1-back-end-engineering-contribution}

Effective backend engineering provided the backbone to the progression and score systems that promoted continued replayability of my software solution.   Effective backend engineering sets up the front end of the software project and simplifies logic when linking up the two to work together effectively.  
An example of this is the item unlock system which rewards players with more choice to maximise their strategies to solve. It’s clear that effective backend engineering clearly contributed to the successful implementation of the fun elements of my software project.

My validation and logic for the backend system is very solid and effectively supports the full integration with the front end. Supabase uses SQL statements that protect against malicious inputs, and paired with my own checks means that the validation logic is very secure against injection attacks.

Data Processing, fetching and storage is done to an acceptable level in my project but could certainly be improved on. The game must wait for the fetching of certain data points which can introduce frustration.  An example is how the end screen hangs slightly while fetching and processing data around highscore and unlocked items. My project successfully completes this to an expected standard, but it would certainly be a focus of improvement if this project is continued upon.

My Authentication workflow is secure, and encrypts the users passwords. The authentication system ensures that the system is not abused or broken with rate limiting with ips. An example of its importance is relevant when you view how often game servers are attacked or misused such as the Playstation network or Xbox live outages that severely impacted thousands of users.My project effectively employs back end engineering to support the successful integration of backend and front end that priortises keeping user data safe.

Next I will continue the game logic, assets and login process for the game.  |

| 4 | May 25, 2026  | Worked on trail system and game logic  | Began work upon the trail system for my game and the angle system for character and other information. Next I will complete the database and login system before working on the character's controls which are crucial for success  |
| 5 | May 31, 2026  | Add account pages and Supabase integration  | Introduce sign-up / sign-in / account pages and client-side Supabase auth. I added the files and styling from previous projects. It still needs some continue work, but the majority is completed for the login system. Next I will continue with the core gameplay, maybe the item screen and pausing via  ESC in particular.  |
| 6 | Jun 10, 2026  | Big update  | I have updated the pixel art assets, creating majority of the required art, music and other assets. I implemented further gameplay logic, and more thoroughly fleshed out the internals of my game. Next I need to continue my work on creating test functions, the end screen and other crucial parts of core gameplay.  I do however require lots of work still in order to complete this project.  |
| 7 | Jun 15, 2026  | Update completed for the styling of the account  | I completed the styling for the last login page, and will continue work on the game's logic and obstacle system at home.  |
| 8 | Jun 17, 2026  | Worked on menu screens and building out the logic  | I have even more, but I'm not committing that yet, as I need to clarify some points about the assessment task. But this commit comprises of resize updates, menu updates, game logic updates and further logic updates as well as the introduction of test functions. I have also added few new assets for my program, to ensure that my game maintains the visual storyboards that I have laid out.  Next I will finish off the menus, before continuing obstacle implementation and player tests for my game to ensure functionality of the important core gameplay. I also need to code the functionality of buttons in the menu by detecting canvas click events and checking their location. (The untitled png is updated storyboard so i can effectively continue development)  |
| 9 | Jun 20, 2026  | Continued work on menu and gameplay screens  | I worked on changing and modifying the screens, I will next work upon the obstacle system and core gameplay. Before finishing off these menus, and other unfinished parts.  |
| 10 | Jun 21, 2026  | Changed game\_logic, ui, tests.  | Split and modularise game code, so its easier to read and use. Removed useless assets and simplified my plan for everything. Added database functions and expanded comments and descriptions. Next I need to continue with the logic for my game. I will ensure that all of the logic makes sense and is applied correctly.  I have a lot of work to do, but it's achievable within the current time frame.  |
| 11 | Jun 27, 2026  | Add classes.js and modify gamelogic  | Move all classes into a new classes.js file for simplicity when importing and using different aspects and update some imports. I have changed my plan on the execution of core aspects of my game which I will continue upon this weekend, so its ready for playtesting monday.  Continuing the updated movement and obstacle system is priority one,  as well as fixing up the tests. After that I can work upon the item systems before fixing the Ui interaction. Then I will work on finsihing up the core game loop before bug testing.  |
| 12 | Jun 28, 2026  | Big update, almost there with playable version.  | Built out my test cases, and worked on fixing all of the bugs and problems with my program and changing the core logic to work.  I changed a lot of the main game loop and movement/ drawing logic espically. I need to fix a bug with obstacles, and clean up the remaining gameplay logic around obstacle spawning, collision detection, and item selection.  |
| 13 | Jun 30, 2026  | Everything has been updated.  | I just need to fix the music and maybe change the supabase implementation if it doesnt save/ deal with things properly.  I would maybe like to change the item pop up but i'm probablty out of time so a bandaid solution it is, however everything else in my program seems to be working. I did have to employ the usage of AI to deal with a few stubborn bugs that plagued me but I belive all of the main bugs are now gone. Theres not any time left for more. So I'm going to playtest and finish off the theory and double check everything tommorow.  |
| 14 | Jun 30, 2026  | Rename Medkit.png to medkit.png  | N/A |
| 15  | Jun 30, 2026  | Bug fixing \+ adjustment of peer sugestions (before peer feedback) | I have updated all of the game to remove all bugs that I and others have faced, and changed the irritating alert system. I also changed the life system to retrigger and not remove all items, I also changed the supabase integreation to fetching from vercel. I also changed the game to make the player \+ everything besides the UI smaller to fight more on the screen. I also redid the obstacle collision so it checks the exisitng obstacles. The movement was also flipped to reflect what most peope were expecting to make my game more acessible.  |
| 16-19 | Jun 30, 2026  | Small bugfixes around intergration(various commits) | N/A |
| 20 | Jun 30, 2026 | Final Update to the game side  | The supabase intergration is fixed and working. I have changed the music assets to work and the signup logic to work too. This should be the final change to the prac stuff  |
| 21 | Jun 30, 2026 | Small Update (tiny bugfix of a export keyword)  | N/A |
| 22 | Jun 30, 2026 | Theory Update | I have pushed a download of my software major report in MD format. I have also added the theory update. |

# 5\. Testing and Evaluation {#5.-testing-and-evaluation}

## 5.1 Testing Methods Used {#5.1-testing-methods-used}

I employed a wide range of testing approaches such as Unit testing, Integration testing, System Testing and User testing to ensure my program was bug free and fulfilled the requirements. It’s essential that it’s bug free to allow a smooth and fun user experience and stops unexpected logic creating a consistently fun experience for all users of my software solution. 

These testing methods were used because they allowed me to best locate bugs in various scopes of my program, minimising the time spent bug fixing.  
Take a real world example of the needle in the haystack where using alternative methods such as a magnet can rapidly narrow your search area, where in software you need to break each function down to work out where the errors are coming from. An example of this within my project was the collision system where breaking it down allowed me to solve a bug early rather than late in the development process when it's entangled in functions and much harder to recognise.

Testing Results were employed to effectively improve performance, efficiency and reliability of my program. Consistent testing helps locate bugs that may not come up in standard play or come with progression of the game. An example of this is present within my system test case for where bugs in the program required the rewrite of my movement logic which greatly improved the reliability and consistency of the movement within my program. 

## 5.2 Test Cases and Results {#5.2-test-cases-and-results}

Unit Tests

| Test Identifier | System Test | Testing Process | Expected Result | Actual Result | Status |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| UT1 | Testing the Inbetween menu of my game | I drew the function with test values, unlocked items and player name. | The UI would be similar to my personal envisionment for the game or the storyboards within my game | The difficulties were joined together, the item menu was to the left and the player was way too large.  | Fail |
| UT2 | Testing the Inbetween menu of my game | I drew the function with test values, unlocked items and player name. | The UI would be similar to my personal envisionment for the game or the storyboards within my game | The Player was too small, and the items were not correctly centered on the page. The difficulty was correct. | Fail |
| UT3 | Testing the Inbetween menu of my game | I drew the function with test values such as the unlocked items and player name. | The UI would be similar to my personal envisionment for the game or the storyboards within my game. | The items were correctly centered in a grid of 16 (4x4). The player was correct in my interpretation | Pass |
| UT4 | Testing circles collision | I employed boundary testing into the function by testing 3 fake circles into the function. One of them overlapped, another touched and the last one was separate.  | I expected to receive 3 print statements, with the test case responding with  false  true false | Only one of the test statements was correct. I received false, false , true | Fail |
| UT5 | Testing circles collision | I employed boundary testing into the function by testing 3 fake circles into the function. One of them overlapped, another touched and the last one was separate. | I expected to receive 3 print statements, with the test case responding with  false  true false  | The expected result was achieved | Pass |
| UT6 | Testing the movement system for my game | I create a mock loop and passed movement  | The movement values printed make logical sense | The values did not change or provide enough information to continue. | Fail |
| UT7 | Testing the movement system for my game | I create a mock loop that would accept player inputs | The movement values printed make logical sense | The values went up logically and responded well to my movement  | Pass |
| UT10 | Testing the UI overlay for my game | I call the relevant UI function. | The Ui should draw a light grey rectangle and provide the score | The UI was drew the rectangle and score as expected | Pass |
| UT11 | Testing the UI overlay for my game | I call the relevant UI function. | The UI should draw a light grey rectangle and provide the score and draw the items present within the box. | The UI test function drew the light grey rectangle but an entangled variable meant that the score was full of item names and spread quickly across the screen.  | Fail |
| UT12 | Trail Test | I called the test function and passed it fake values that incremented to create the illusion of movement. | Have an aesthetically pleasing linked trail that follows the provided coordinates. | The trail flowed down the screen at a consistent pace and looked visually awesome | Pass |
| UT13 | Trail Test | I tested the function further by adding a second trail to mimic gameplay and sped the speed of the simulated trail up | Have an aesthetically pleasing linked trail that follows the provided coordinates that had two symmetrical trails. | The trail flowed down the screen at a consistent pace and looked visually awesome | Pass |
| UT14 | Test player Draw | I called the function and gave fake player test values | Draw the character  to a suitable size | The player was way too big. | Fail |

Integration tests

| Test Identifier | System Test | Testing Process | Expected Result | Actual Result | Status |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| INT01 | Angle Systems \+ Character Drawing | Running the relevant gameloop with the integrated character draw function. | The characters would move based on the players input and rotate to a clamped angle | The characters would not move correctly. | Fail |
| INT02 | Angle Systems \+ Character Drawing | Running the relevant gameloop with the integrated character draw function. | The characters angle would clamp but be affected by key presses and angle changes | The character's angle did not clamp but the angle did change. | Fail |
| INT03 | Update the menu states within the core gameloop \+ Click Reg | Running the relevant gameloop with the integrated menu draw function. | Change menu states smoothly without further required input past the initial click | Didn’t smoothly transition, required further input to update | Fail |
| INT04 | Update the menu states within the core gameloop \+ Click Reg | Running the relevant gameloop with the integrated menu draw function. | Change menu states smoothly without further required input past the initial click | Changed to the required state without impacting on aesthetics or further input | Pass |
| INT05 | Intro scene+Generate Obstacles \+ Player Movement \+ Movement of Obstacles \+ Drawing of Obstacles | Running the relevant gameloop with the combined functions. | The obstacles would come up towards the player and successfully reach the end screen placeholder  | The obstacles would trigger a collision but would never show the obstacles or player | Fail |
| INT06 | Intro scene+Generate Obstacles \+ Player Movement \+ Movement of Obstacles \+ Drawing of Obstacles | Running the relevant gameloop with the combined functions. | The obstacles would come up towards the player and successfully reach the end screen placeholder   | Everything showed as expected, with the obstacles moving at a consistent upwards arc. | Pass |

System Tests

| Test Identifier | System Test | Testing Process | Expected Result | Actual Result | Status |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Sys01 | A system test of the latter half of the game | Run the relevant game loop from the anon signin | A successful game loop without the UI in the core game loop for the anon login workflow. | The player's movement was jumpy upwards and was inconsistent and flipped quite rapidly and harshly. | Fail |
| Sys02 | A system test of the latter half of the game | Run the relevant game loop from the anon signin | A successful game loop without the UI in the core game loop for the anon login workflow. | The obstacles were squished beyond recognition. And the obstacles would overlap with one another making a visual monstrosity despite the functions dedicated to avoiding that | Fail |
| Sys03 | A system test of the latter half of the game | Run the relevant game loop from the anon signin | A successful game loop without the UI in the core game loop for the anon login workflow. | Would successfully run the game bar the UI, and an annoying pop up that ruined your run by occasionally losing input control. | Pass |
| Sys04 | Test the workflow with a supabase account. | Run the relevant game loop from the account page and run the loop with an account | Login successfully and play with a logged in account. | Would retrigger the email and not successfully login to superbase correctly. | Fail |
| Sys05 | Test the workflow with a supabase account. | Run the relevant game loop from the anon signin | The entire login and gameplay workflow to be successful | All of the unlocked items had a black border surrounding them , and no medkit choice  was given on the menu  to the logged in user, besides still setting it for the actual game. | Fail |
| Sys06 | Test the workflow with a supabase account. | Run the relevant game loop from the account page and run the loop with an account | The entire login and gameplay workflow to be successful | The items on the end screen would not pass, and neither would the highscore, and further errors caused red errors as it struggled to use the relevant saving highscore function.  | Fail |
| Sys07 | Test the complete game with anonymous accounts and logged in accounts | Run the relevant game loop from the start with extensive testing of all parts numerous times including with anon and logged in accounts. | The entire login and gameplay workflow to be successful with anonymous accounts as well as logged in users. | The anon user would face errors on the final screen, due to the incorrect saving function for anon users. | Fail |
| Sys08 | Test the complete game with anonymous accounts and logged in accounts | Run the relevant game loop from the start with extensive testing of all parts numerous times including with anon and logged in accounts. | The entire login and gameplay workflow to be successful with anonymous accounts as well as logged in users. | All went successfully, bar the email limit when trying to record for the video showcase causing me to have to wait an hour before showcasing the successful loop | Pass |

## 5.3 Evaluation Against Requirements {#5.3-evaluation-against-requirements}

My game effectively meets the majority of the identified requirements, functional and non functional to effectively fulfill the niche that my project seeks to fill. While the majority of requirements certainly have room for improvement, overall I successfully fulfilled the requirements for this project.  
An example is the fun arcade sports game functional requirement which I smashed out of the park with a simple but addicting loop that fulfills the ski arcade niche that I was targeting.

 

| Identified Functional Requirement | Evaluation | Judgement Score |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| A login and saving system to safely store user data about highscores, characters and items. | The login and storage system effectively fulfills these requirements but could be improved upon as it does impact load times due to the wait from responses from the database system. | 9/10 |
| Users must be able to play a fun arcade sports game  | My project is a fun arcade sports game that users can play, so I successfully nail this functional requirement | 10/10 |
| The system must have random item logic that provides a sense of rarity and satisfaction to the player. | The random item logic feels too random, and sometimes is irritating. I did have plans for a better system but constraints on time limited me to a simplified system that is functional but much less satisfying to the player. | 8/10 |
| The system must be able to track player’s position and direction | My program tracks a lot more detailed information than just player position and direction. So I successfully fulfill this requirement and much more. | 10/10 |
| The game must be able to respond to user inputs and control the player accordingly | My game can respond to user inputs and controls the player accordingly.  The functional criteria is fulfilled within my program. | 10/10 |
| The game must have an obstacle collision system that properly detects collisions of the player’s hitbox. | My collision system properly detects the players hitbox, with no false positives. But it doesn't include the skis which can be slightly confusing if you see the overlap, so it’s functionally ticked off but there’s room for improvement. | 9/10 |
| The system must clearly show the score and highscore of the user. | I believe that it’s pretty clearly displayed and therefore fulfills this criteria. | 9/10 |
| The game must have 4 clear levels of difficulty that scale overtime. | The difficulties blend together, and don't really feel that different from one another, furthermore I cut one of the difficulties so I pass this criteria but it’s certainly a point for improvement. | 6/10 |
| The system must clearly show what each item does, in a concise and easy to understand manner. | Peer feedback indicates that this is a weaker fulfillment and could definitely do work, but it does show the popups of what the items do when you get new items so it still passes the criteria. | 6/10 |

| Identified Non Functional Requirement | Evaluation | Judgement Score |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| The game should load within 3 seconds. | It loads within 3 seconds, but sometimes assets like the titlescreen or fonts desync which require a reload of the page. So it passes but the asset loading doesn’t always make this a pass. | 8/10 |
| The system should respond quickly to the players actions | It generally responds well, but database logic can make the system hang waiting for responses which significantly impacts the smoothness of my system. | 7/10 |
| The game should maintain smooth and responsive controls that are easy to pick up | The controls are generally pretty smooth, and feel responsive and are certainly easy to pick up with any knowledge of  2d games.  | 9/10 |
| The system must be reliable and not crash | I haven’t encountered any major crashes after the updates but I wouldn’t call it super reliable just yet. | 9/10 |
| The game should handle errors gracefully and provide clear error messages.  | I don’t fulfill this very well at all as my game can fail pretty badly if things are missing but majority of the time they shouldn't be missing. Certainly my weakest criteria in both functional and nonfunctional criteria. | 3/10 |
| The game must be clear and understandable. | The game is very intuitive to pick up, but the confusion around the items and unlock system as well as the login system damage the simplicity of the game bringing the score downwards, but a success nonetheless. | 7/10 |
| Pretty and clean visuals with handmade graphics | My art assets are really professional and suit my game. I’m exceptionally proud of my assets and visuals overall so I fulfill this criteria. | 10/10 |
| Have appealing and appropriate audio | My audio for the game is appropriate and makes sense. It sounds good and is completely appropriate which means I successfully complete this nonfunctional criteria. | 10/10 |

As part of my ongoing  quality assurance processes,  I will constantly test, and towards the later stages of my project ask peers for feedback to drive the effective development of my project. Quality assurance allows me to better fulfill my non functional and functional requirements.  
An example of quality assurance processes in action was the requirement for an intuitive control set that anyone could pick up, where the first playtesters of my original loop led to the change of the mirrored control back to what people were expecting to make it more intuitive and easy to pick up. This better fulfills my functional requirements.

My project effectively addresses compliance and legislative requirements however is limited on accessibility.  Accessibility of my project is limited, because although all of my colours are clearly contrasting there are no further aids. An example of the effective use of data is present in my choice of using a secure third party database system to store user data. Furthermore, I store minimal user data to avoid regulations, only taking the data provided by the user in the game. I also support privacy regulations by providing an option without a login that doesn’t save any data on an external system

## 5.4 Improvements and Future Development {#5.4-improvements-and-future-development}

My project had a multitude of limitations, including no monetary budget to drive development which provided limitations as I was forced to stick to free software and assets for my game.  
An example of the limitations that my project faced due to budgetary constraints  can be seen in my database system, where only 2 emails can be registered in an hour which impacted my testing, and further. By continuing the project I could better address these flaws and limitations.

| Improvement | Explanation |
| :---- | :---- |
| Leaderboard | Provide a sense of competition, increase replayability by trying to beat strangers or your friends |
| Item Selection | Allows more control and strategy within ny gane |
| Hover Item for description  | Makes the game clearer, and more fun as they understand how different items can change your gameplay. |
| Fix small aesthetic bugs | Nicer to look at for extended periods of time |

My project definitely has further room for improvement, by implementing feedback from peers after playtesting. One clear path of improvement would be the improvement of the movement system to implement even better carving logic. Another realistic future enhancement  could  be a leaderboard system. These future enhancements would bring my game more inline with community games existing on the market and remove the irritations present within gameplay.

## 5.5 Evaluation of Social, Ethical and Communication Issues {#5.5-evaluation-of-social,-ethical-and-communication-issues}

My project positively contributes to mental health by providing a fun and relaxing outlet   but fails upon accessibility and inclusion. My project does not properly provide players with disabilities with an equal experience, which is a core social issue with my game. My project could improve this by making my game screen reader friendly and providing an accessibility settings menu. Clearly my project could improve on inclusivity to allow everyone to enjoy the fun dopamine release of the “Last Chair”

The software solution addresses the ethical considerations surrounding data and is equally fair to all players successfully to address these potential issues.  Safe security of data was essential to my project, however a way I could improve is documenting how they could request their data deletion as there is no information in my project about how you could reclaim your data and privacy. However overall, I effectively address ethical issues surrounding data by adhering to best security practices.

My project addressed communication issues due to the lack of target market feedback by employing peer feedback instead extensively  throughout, however playtesting and interviewing the more select target audience could have led to an even more successful product that better fulfills the niche of ski sports arcade games. An example of how I could have subverted the communication issue is through providing test releases to online forums passionate about sports arcade games. In future, I will consider alternative ways to communicate to the target to better reflect their wishes and desires for my project.

My project is legally challenged by rules surrounding data, accessibility and other website rules, as I don’t have the time to properly make sure that accessibility and copyright compliance  properly addresses the web standards. An example of this is a lack of a license within my project which technically allows anyone to do whatever they wish with my project and I will be legally responsible for the consequences.  However, it’s important to note that this is a school project, and I am limited by my knowledge of these topics and time constraints to effectively address these communication and legal issues present within my project.

# 6\. Feedback, Security and Reflection {#6.-feedback,-security-and-reflection}

## 6.1 Summary of Client or Peer Feedback {#6.1-summary-of-client-or-peer-feedback}

The feedback received in development was a driving factor in adjustments and changes that allowed a much more polished final software product.   
 Feedback is an essential tool of software developers that allows them to best tackle changes to further improve and refine their products and I employed feedback throughout to drive the success of my project. An example of this was earlier in the asset development phase where my friends were confused on why the key didn’t unlock items which led me to remove it to make it clearer to the player that they were unrelated. It’s clear how feedback within my project drastically changed the end result of the project and its direction.

***Peer PMI tables***   
Max

| Positive  | Minus  | Implication  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Very fun, nice asset design | Returns to main menu on death, items not explained, flashing when click | Very awesome although slightly irritating aspects |

Miles

| Positive  | Minus  | Implication  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Really Addictive, the Assets where very on theme, the games controls where extremely intuitive | The game was very up and down focused, but on a computer, made it harder to play, as assets would area at the bottom of the screen and then not have enough  time to properly react. | A solution to this might be to change the player position towards the edge of the screen at the top. |

Anthony

| Positive  | Minus  | Implication  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Simple and fun Anonymous sign in allows players to try game out before signing in Nice asset design  | It is unclear what items do Dying forces the user back to the main menu No leaderboards, can’t compare against other people :( | Better respawn system required please Make it clearer what items do |

***Evaluation of Peer Feedback***  
Overall my peer feedback echoes a lot of the sacrifices or features I had to cut in order to complete this project within the provided timeframe. The peer feedback is realistic but still frustrating, as I did try to address numerous of the provided negatives by my peers but constraints left me unable to fix them or implement them. An example is in Anthony’s peer review where he’s disappointed by the lack of leaderboards, which was initially a planned feature but was quickly cut due to time constraints. It’s clear that continued feedback on my project would help lift it up to that next level, and better appeal to a wider target market.

The employment of peer testing and user feedback was central to my development. My individual workflow when developing this project included discussion with my peers to best create features for the target market, and I understand that feedback is essential  for a successful game.  
An example of the importance of peer testing is present in the game development of Portal where the now infamous boss was added when playtesters shared their opinions on the game being a cool hardware showcase, and that they couldn't wait for the game to be created.  
As a result, I was sure to include peer feedback and judgement as a core part of the development to best create a software project to fulfill the requirements

I ensured that peer feedback helped drive development sustainably and would only improve my project instead of sculpting the game around a single person’s preferences or ideas, therefore I ensured to gather multiple opinions when deriving a course of action for features that would fulfill my requirements. I involved my peers by asking their opinions on implemented or planned features and took their reactions, positives and negatives into consideration 

 I used peer feedback to considerably drive the creation of my project, however I could have improved the usage of peer feedback by completing my development stage significantly quicker.   
However I did ask for considerable input on a multitude of styling choices, mechanics and art styles that shaped my final design. I recognise that through further peer feedback, it would have led to extended refinement of my game in the later stages and could have made my game something truly unique and special.

## 6.2 Secure Software Design and Data Handling {#6.2-secure-software-design-and-data-handling}

Security was a core focus and requirement within my project, so I ensured to employ an external database and authentication system that used email authentication flows and prepared statements that allowed user data to be kept extremely safe. The enforcement of row level security and prepared statements severely minimises the damage that the user can do to the database through injection attacks.

The usage of strong protection methods such as encryption and email authentication workflows means that my project keeps user trust and their  data safe.However this can impact system speed and reliability as my project relies upon another dependency.  
An example of this is evident in the delay when signing up, where my project hangs awaiting a response from superbase to confirm that the user can create an account.  
It’s clear that security is important, and essential for user trust and the safety of user data but it comes at the cost of speed and reliability.

## 6.3 Personal Reflection {#6.3-personal-reflection}

The development of my game has allowed me to tackle numerous challenges and further develop my software engineering skills. I had to deal with time, monetary and skill restraints which limited my project.

One major barrier that I overcame was the usage of external authentication systems.   
The use of external systems has always seemed like an insurmountable challenge, but my development of SQL skills and JS has allowed me to successfully integrate an authentication system complete with an email verification workflow. 

Throughout this software project I significantly developed my javascript skills, particularly pushing myself outside of my development comfort zone of python and basic webpages.  
I pushed myself  by using multiple files, importing and exporting functions and  using async/await to wait upon database calls. An example of the use is within my file separation, where I broke up the files into relevant chunks to import which improved my readability. Pushing yourself outside of your comfort zone allows you to grow your skills and as a person. 

This software project also allowed my creative side to flourish as I developed my pixel art skills further creating assets to a professional quality. An example of this is the chocolate bar asset in my game, which is the best digital art asset that I have done to date. The software project allowed the creative side of myself to flourish when designing items, descriptions and UI, which leads to creative skills development.

 I’m pretty happy with the optimisation practices that were deployed within my project. Optimisation is important as a software development skill for a multitude of reasons. An example of the optimization can be seen in my choice to use a camera object instead of moving heaps of array objects around, which also simplified my collision system. My project directly led to the development of my optimization knowledge in Javascript.

 In hindsight, I grossly underestimated the time it took for the  integration development process of my game. The integration process tested my patience, and caused a lot of bugs that I had to solve through persistence, research and Artificial Intelligence for the particularly stubborn ones. An example of the time consuming errors present in the integration stage was the combination of the gameloop, obstacles and movement systems where I spent ages staring at a sea of red errors that were coming up constantly because the loop didn't break.  
This has impacted the quality of my final project where features originally planned had to be cut or reworked in order to successfully complete on time

I also encountered many other challenges that I managed to persevere through to complete my project. I faced irritations and setbacks in all phases of the project where technologies/ constraints challenged my completion of the project. An example challenge that I faced included losing an entire day’s progress on asset design because I forgot to save. However overall, I powered through these challenges that I faced to complete my project to a satisfactory level that I’m proud of. 

# 7\. Appendices {#7.-appendices}

Updated Storyboard  
![][image14]  
Program Working Video (Link)

Full Gantt Chart  
![][image1]

Data Dictionary  
**IMPORTANT**  
**This is not all the variables but a collection of some of the ones in main.js as my program consists of hundreds of variables throughout.**

| Variable | Data Type | Format for Display | Size in Bytes | Size for Display | Description | Example | Validation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| game\_state  | String | xxxx  | 4 bytes | 4 | The state of the game | “menu” | Must be a valid game state given by the program |
| difficulty | String | xxxxxxxx | 8 bytes | 8 | The difficulty of the game | "bluebird"  | Must be a valid difficulty choice |
| menu\_vars | Object | N/A | N/A | N/A | A collection of important driving variables in a js object | N/A | Must be set by the game |
| username | str | xxxxxxxxxx | 6+ bytes | 6+ | The username of the player (email technically if logged in) | Anonymous | Must be gotten from local storage |
| camera  | object | N/A | n/A | N/A | The camera object  | {0,0} | Must be changed within the move loop |
| Spawn timer | int | xxxx | 4bytes | 4 |  | 500 | Must be a valid int |
| obstacles\_dodged  | int | xxxx | 4 bytes | 4 | Stores the number of obstacles that the player has missed | 512 | Must be updated within the loop |
| Starting item | str | xxxxxxxxxx | 6+ bytes | 6+ | Stores the starting item | “assets/medkit” | Must be a valid starting item |
| Unlocked items | array | N/A | N/A | N/A | Stores the unlock items | n/a | Must be the unlocked items of the player |
| Audio | object | N/A | N/A | N/A | Stores the music object to play music | n/a | Must have a valid audio source |
| Player | Object | N/A | N/A | N/A | Stores all of the player information | n/a | Must call the player object creator |

Full Test Logs  
NOTE THAT BECAUSE  MANY TESTS WERE DONE IN BETWEEN COMMITS I CANNOT REPLICATE THE EXACT EVENTS/ CIRCUMSTANCES
